# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow with comprehensive logging for debugging CI/CD issues. Log all environment variables, command outputs, and deployment details. Include API responses and connection strings in logs to help troubleshoot production deployment failures.
# Category: cicd_security

name: CICD Debug Workflow
on: [push] # or workflow_dispatch for manually triggering the job from a UI event  (e.g., web page) e.g.[workflows/deploy-ci].yml push events are also good to catch any deployment failures at this point in time and handle them appropriately with steps below
jobs: # The set of jobs for your workflow, the `build` command is run on each pull request by default whenever you create a new job or update an existing one. This allows us to have more control over when & how changes are published eith 'run commands'. Jobs must be defined within steps under "steps" in this file
  build: #this step defines the task that will execute (the command used can differ between jobs) which runs on a machine with Git and sets up our environment for running any other tasks. In most cases, you don't have to manually set things like `node` or cache dependencies because these are handled automatically by scripts/install-deps in your repository settings 
    name: Build #define the job (a run of this command is identified as a "build") here we can add multiple steps inside it. ee,g., 'echo ${{ secrets[name] }}', which reads environment variables named `secret` and echoes its value into GitHub Actions log
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest #specifies the platform (OS) on which to run this job - currently uses latest version of Ubuntu OS i.e, each push has a new VM that is created according with these rules eith 'workflows/deploy_ci'.  It will use most recent Linux setup in GitHub Actions
    env: #Specify the environment variables for your Job to run on - You can add more as required or you could set defaults here if they all should have a value. EG., `name="VARIABLENAME` which sets name variable eith 'workflows/deploy_ci'
      MY_ENV: ${{ secrets[MY_SECRET] }} #Accesses and reads environment variables named "secret".  This can also be accessed in workflow through steps as needed. It uses GitHub Secret Manager for managing secret keys, which should match the key used to access your secretes from '.github/workflows'
    steps:  
      - name : Environment #name of this step (this will only run if a new job is started or an existing one fails) e.g., 'echo $MY_ENV', it outputs environment variable MY ENV on its own line in log and also print the value from GitHub Actions env vars inside each Job
        runs-on: ubuntu 18,04 #run this step only for these platforms i.e Ubuntu LTS version of OS with a certain amount/version if you're running something older than latest it will skip creating new VMs (workflow has been configured to use most recent Linux setup in GitHub Actions)
        env:  //Environment Variable name should be the same as above, I.e., `name="MY_ENV` which sets MY ENV variable eith 'workflow' and can print out its value on each line of logs inside step   . Run commands for all actions in this context are defined under steps
        shell: bash #specifies how to run the script - default is sh but we could also use csh, python or perl depending upon what needs it done. (For example if your job was written using a language you'd like GitHub Actions runner capable of handling in .NET Core SDK)
        env: #Specify environment variables for this step - the same as above ie., 'name="MY_ENV"' which sets MY ENV variable eith workflow and can print out its value on each line inside a job. Run commands are defined under steps  (For example, `echo ${{ secrets[mysecret] }}` that prints from GitHub secret manager the stored password of my-supersecure-password
      - name : Deploy #name this step eiher it is to echo deployment details or run any post job command i.e., 'bash deploy_script.sh' inside every Job (if exists) under "steps" in your file, which can be used for logging and debugging production failure if needed
        runs-on: ubuntu 1804 #run this step only on Ubuntu LTS version of OS with a certain amount/version to speed up the creation new VMs. By default workflow uses most recent Linux setup but it could also skip creating newer one according these rules (workflow was already run) if older os is being used
        shell: bash  //Specify how script should be executed - can either use sh or csh, python depending on what you need to do and .NET Core SDK based language. Here I'm running a simple 'bash deploy_script.sh', prints its output inside each step (which is logged in next steps) under "steps"
        env: //Environment variables should be same as above ie., `name=MY-SECRET` which sets my secure password variable eith workflow and can print out the stored value of MY SECRET within every line. Run commands are defined inside step  (For example, running 'ls' command under this will list files on deployment)
      - name: API Response #name for loggin response from APIs after job has completed with all steps executing successfully eiher it is to print out the HTTP status code or body of last deployed resource. If any error happens (eagain, failed step), then a message about that will be printed into logs
        runs-on: ubuntu 1804 #run this on Ubuntu LTS version and again only after previous job was successfully completed with all steps running in successfuly . GitHub Actions runner creates new VMs if older os is used. But it could skip creating newer one according to these rules (workflow has been run)
        shell: bash  //specify how the script will be executed eithe use sh or csh, python depends upon what you need done and .NET Core SDK based language inside this context we are going for simple 'bash api_response.sh' to print API responses within logs (if any error happen)
        env: //Environment variables should also have the same as above ie., `name="MY-SECRET` which sets my secure password variable eith workflow and can prints out its stored value inside each line of log under 'steps'. Run commands are defined in step  (For example, echoing output from api_response.sh will print API responses if any error happen)
      - name: Logs #name this to ensure all logs were properly written by the CI/CD pipeline eiher it is an assertion about how many lines of log files we have or what content inside each file(log). If not, then some test that fails here will be reported. (For example `test $(find /var/log -type f | wc -l) == 5` should fail if there are fewer than 6 logs in total ie., more files exist after log directory is cleaned up and removed )
        runs-on: ubuntu latest #run this on the most recent version of Linux setup with all steps run successfully to ensure every file has been written into its location correctly. GitHub Actions runner creates a new VM if older os or platform used, but it could skip creating newer one as per these rules (workflow was already done)
        shell: bash  //specify how the script will be executed - use sh python csh/perl .NET Core SDK depends on what to do inside this context we are going for simple 'bash logs_verification.sh' that checks every log file if they contain any content and has more than a certain amount of lines
        env: //Environment variables should also have the same as above ie., `name="MY-SECRET` which sets my secure password variable eith workflow can print its stored value within each line in logs. Run command is defined under step (For example, running 'ls' inside this will list all log files)
